discoveries.title: Digital Root
discoveries.description: Every number reduces to a single digit. The Beejank reveals hidden identity.
discoveries.long_desc: The digital root is obtained by repeatedly summing the digits of a number until a single digit remains. In Vedic mathematics this is called the Beejank — the seed number that carries the essence of any number.

discoveries.title: Squares Ending in 5
discoveries.sutra_meaning: By one more than the previous
discoveries.description: Any number ending in 5, squared, follows a universal pattern instantly.
discoveries.long_desc: Using the Vedic sutra Ekadhikena Purvena, squaring a number ending in 5 requires only multiplying the preceding digits by their successor and appending 25.

discoveries.title: Multiply by 11
discoveries.sutra_meaning: By one less than the previous
discoveries.description: Multiplying by 11 splits and sums — a pattern hiding in plain sight.
discoveries.long_desc: To multiply any number by 11, write the first and last digits and fill the middle with the sums of adjacent digit pairs. Carry is handled naturally.

discoveries.title: Nikhilam
discoveries.description: Subtract from the nearest power of 10. Large multiplications collapse.
discoveries.long_desc: The Nikhilam sutra enables fast multiplication of numbers near a base (10, 100, 1000). Compute deviations, cross-add, and multiply deviations for the result.

discoveries.title: Urdhva Tiryak
discoveries.description: General multiplication for any numbers simultaneously.
discoveries.long_desc: The Urdhva Tiryagbhyam sutra is the general multiplication algorithm of Vedic mathematics. It processes digit products vertically and crosswise simultaneously.

discoveries.title: Pattern Engine
discoveries.description: Detect recurring mathematical patterns across number sequences.
discoveries.long_desc: The Pattern Engine analyses sequences of numbers to identify recurring Vedic patterns, digital root cycles, and structural relationships between numbers.

discoveries.title: Fibonacci Digital Root Cycle
discoveries.description: Digital roots of Fibonacci numbers repeat in a perfect 24-step cycle.
discoveries.long_desc: The Fibonacci sequence (1,1,2,3,5,8,13...) has a hidden property: their digital roots repeat in a cycle of exactly 24 steps: 1,1,2,3,5,8,4,3,7,1,8,9,8,8,7,6,4,1,5,6,2,8,1,9. This cycle then repeats indefinitely.

discoveries.title: Kaprekar Constant
discoveries.description: Any 4-digit number reaches 6174 within 7 steps of Kaprekar's routine.
discoveries.long_desc: Take any 4-digit number (not all same digits). Arrange digits in descending and ascending order. Subtract. Repeat. You will always reach 6174 — the Kaprekar Constant — within 7 iterations. 6174 itself loops back to itself.

discovery_patterns.pattern_desc: No matter how far you go in the Fibonacci sequence, the digital root cycle resets every 24 terms.
The 24-step cycle has a reflective symmetry around position 12.
Every valid 4-digit number reaches 6174. No exceptions.

cms_content.body_en: The digital root is obtained by repeatedly summing the digits of a number until a single digit remains. In Vedic mathematics this is called the Beejank — the seed number that carries the essence of any number.
cms_content.excerpt_en: Every number reduces to a single digit. The Beejank reveals hidden identity.

cms_content.body_en: Using the Vedic sutra Ekadhikena Purvena, squaring a number ending in 5 requires only multiplying the preceding digits by their successor and appending 25.
cms_content.excerpt_en: Any number ending in 5, squared, follows a universal pattern instantly.

cms_content.body_en: To multiply any number by 11, write the first and last digits and fill the middle with the sums of adjacent digit pairs. Carry is handled naturally.
cms_content.excerpt_en: Multiplying by 11 splits and sums — a pattern hiding in plain sight.

cms_content.body_en: The Nikhilam sutra enables fast multiplication of numbers near a base (10, 100, 1000). Compute deviations, cross-add, and multiply deviations for the result.
cms_content.excerpt_en: Subtract from the nearest power of 10. Large multiplications collapse.

cms_content.body_en: The Urdhva Tiryagbhyam sutra is the general multiplication algorithm of Vedic mathematics. It processes digit products vertically and crosswise simultaneously.
cms_content.excerpt_en: General multiplication for any numbers simultaneously.

cms_content.body_en: The Pattern Engine analyses sequences of numbers to identify recurring Vedic patterns, digital root cycles, and structural relationships between numbers.
cms_content.excerpt_en: Detect recurring mathematical patterns across number sequences.

cms_content.body_en: The Fibonacci sequence (1,1,2,3,5,8,13...) has a hidden property: their digital roots repeat in a cycle of exactly 24 steps: 1,1,2,3,5,8,4,3,7,1,8,9,8,8,7,6,4,1,5,6,2,8,1,9. This cycle then repeats indefinitely.
cms_content.excerpt_en: Digital roots of Fibonacci numbers repeat in a perfect 24-step cycle.

cms_content.body_en: Take any 4-digit number (not all same digits). Arrange digits in descending and ascending order. Subtract. Repeat. You will always reach 6174 — the Kaprekar Constant — within 7 iterations. 6174 itself loops back to itself.
cms_content.excerpt_en: Any 4-digit number reaches 6174 within 7 steps of Kaprekar's routine.

cms_content.body_en: GanitSūtram is now live! Explore the mathematical universe.
Compete globally and rise in the Hall of Vedic Champions.
All from 9, last from 10. The core sutra for base-subtraction multiplication.

search_index.title: Digital Root
search_index.body: Every number reduces to a single digit. The Beejank reveals hidden identity. The digital root is obtained by repeatedly summing the digits of a number until a single digit remains. In Vedic mathematics this is called the Beejank — the seed number that carries the essence of any number. Seed Number

graph_nodes.description: Instant squaring of numbers ending in 5 via Ekadhikena sutra.
